claim_under_test: ARGX-119 (MuSK agonist antibody) as an exercise mimetic for age-related sarcopenia is a not-yet-proposed connection.

near_misses_vetted.what: Review: NMJ as a molecular target in sarcopenia; discusses agrin-MuSK disruption.
near_misses_vetted.why_not_this: Shares the PREMISE but names NO specific MuSK agonist and no ARGX-119; frames exercise as an ADJUNCT to combine with NMJ agents, not a pharmacological MIMIC of exercise. My increment: a specific real clinical-stage agonist + exercise-mimetic framing + signature-reversal grounding.

near_misses_vetted.what: Review: agrin-LRP4-MuSK as therapeutic target; lists sarcopenia among NMJ diseases.
near_misses_vetted.why_not_this: Predates ARGX-119; explicitly states enhancing postsynaptic NMJ signaling is desirable but 'no currently available drug has this functionality' — i.e. names the gap I now fill with a real compound.

near_misses_vetted.what: Cell screen: small molecules enhancing MuSK phosphorylation / AChR clustering (4 hits, 8-30x, > disulfiram).
near_misses_vetted.why_not_this: Supports feasibility of the NEXT-GEN small-molecule extension; mentions sarcopenia only in passing; no in vivo aging model, no exercise-mimetic framing, no named clinical compound.

near_misses_vetted.what: NT-1654 soluble neural agrin fragment improves NMJ-disassembly muscle pathology.
near_misses_vetted.why_not_this: Acts UPSTREAM (supplies agrin ligand), still susceptible to neurotrypsin cleavage; preclinical only; framed for muscle-wasting disease, not aging exercise-mimicry.

adjacent_but_distinct: MuSK agonist antibodies (ARGX-119) are in clinical development for CMS/ALS/SMA (all rare NMJ diseases); CAF is an established sarcopenia biomarker. The target biology and the compound both exist — only the CONNECTION (this compound, this indication, this framing) is unclaimed.
independent_confirmation: Open Targets: MuSK's disease-association landscape (1417 diseases) is dominated by NMJ disease (the only non-neuromuscular top association is diabetes mellitus, 0.280); sarcopenia/age-related muscle loss are ABSENT — the target-disease edge I propose is not in the world's largest target-disease resource.
